headline: CATTLE CRASH 2%, GRAINS INCH HIGHER
subheadline: Processing lockout hits livestock while grain funds rotate back after China reset.
lead: Live cattle dropped $4.85 to $240.15 and feeders fell $9.15 to $356.52 as the Cargill Fort Morgan lockout entered day two. The 1,700-worker shutdown is forcing cattle into a bottleneck exactly when fed supplies were already tight. Meanwhile, corn gained 1¾ cents to $4.63 and beans added 4 cents to $11.98 as funds rotated back into grains after this week's China commitment reset. The divergence tells you everything: livestock is fighting logistics, grains are pricing the next leg.
the_takeaway: Processing squeeze hits cattle harder than grain funds can lift corn.
teaser: Cattle crash on Cargill lockout while grains find quiet buying interest.

one_number.unit: workers locked out at Cargill Fort Morgan
one_number.context: The beef processing bottleneck is forcing cattle into a supply squeeze that's hitting feeders harder than live cattle on timing. Fort Morgan processes 6,000 head daily, and with fed cattle supplies already tight, the lockout is creating a backup that's repricing the entire complex. This is structural disruption, not weather noise.

yesterdays_call.summary: Processing bottleneck hits feeders harder than live cattle on timing.
yesterdays_call.note: Feeders dropped 2.5% versus live cattle's 2.0%, exactly the differential the timing suggested.

sections.title: Grains Find Quiet Buying
sections.body: Corn closed $4.63, up 1¾ cents on fund rotation that's been building since Monday's China commitment. **The carry's working again**, December corn at $4.86 is 23 cents over the nearby, wider than it's been in three weeks. Beans added 4 cents to $11.98 on similar positioning, but the move lacks conviction. Meal surged $4.30 to $330.60 on tighter crush margins while oil held flat at $74.01. Planting pace is on schedule across the Belt, no weather premium in the board yet.
sections.catalyst: Fund positioning shifts after China's $17 billion ag commitment earlier this week
sections.vs_yesterday: Grains: buying interest emerged after yesterday's flat drift

sections.title: Livestock Crashes on Processing Jam
sections.body: Live cattle lost $4.85 to $240.15 and feeders dropped $9.15 to $356.52 as the **Cargill Fort Morgan lockout entered day two**. The 1,700-worker shutdown is processing 6,000 fewer head daily, forcing cattle into a backup that's repricing the entire complex. Feeders took the bigger hit on timing: they're further from processing but closer to the margin squeeze. Hogs fell $1.77 to $100.15 on technical selling that followed the cattle collapse. No resolution in sight at Fort Morgan.
sections.catalyst: Cargill Fort Morgan lockout continues, 1,700 workers out second day
sections.bottom_line: Processing bottleneck reprices cattle faster than weather ever could.
sections.vs_yesterday: Livestock: processing crisis deepened exactly as structural risk suggested

sections.title: Energy Holds Iran Premium
sections.body: WTI crude dropped $1.58 to $95.90 as **diplomatic hopes around Hormuz tensions** took some edge off the risk premium. But Barclays kept their $100 Brent forecast and warned of upside risk from inventory draws in what they're calling the worst supply disruption in history. Natural gas fell 5 cents to $3.08 on mild weather forecasts. The first Saudi crude cargo since the crisis began is heading to Japan next week, a sign that some Strait traffic is moving again.
sections.catalyst: Diplomatic progress on Iran-Hormuz tensions, first crude shipment to Japan since crisis
sections.bottom_line: Risk premium easing but structural disruption math unchanged.

outside_the_pit.title: China's Retaliatory Tariffs Cost US Ag $15 Billion
outside_the_pit.body: North Dakota State study puts hard numbers on the trade war damage: $14.9 billion in lost ag export sales over 12 months. That's context for why this week's China commitment reset mattered enough to move fund positioning back toward grains.

outside_the_pit.title: USDA Enhances Livestock Insurance for 2027
outside_the_pit.body: New rules expand coverage and update eligibility as the cattle herd sits at historic lows. The July Cattle Inventory report will show whether meaningful expansion is starting, but insurance changes suggest USDA expects volatility to persist.

outside_the_pit.title: EU Strikes Deal to Remove US Goods Tariffs
outside_the_pit.body: Council and Parliament agreement eliminates remaining tariffs on US products. Another piece of trade normalization that could shift ag export flows as global supply chains reset around Hormuz disruptions.

spread_to_watch.label: Live cattle / feeder ratio
spread_to_watch.commentary: The processing squeeze is compressing the ratio as feeders fall harder than live cattle. When the ratio drops below 0.67, it's historically signaled that feeder premiums have overcorrected. We're almost there.

basis.headline: Eastern Belt corn basis firming on ethanol restart
basis.body: Corn basis is **tightening east of the Mississippi** as ethanol plants come back online after maintenance. Producers with old-crop bushels in storage have a window the futures board isn't fully pricing. Western Belt staying soft, consistent with the seasonal pattern.

weekly_thread.question: Will China's $17 billion ag commitment shift fund positioning back into grains this week?
weekly_thread.status_text: Yes, but quietly. Fund rotation into grains has been building since Monday, showing up in carry structure more than headline moves. The commitment reset positioning, it didn't trigger a rally.

the_more_you_know.title: The processing paradox: why 1,700 workers crash cattle 2%
the_more_you_know.body: Today's $4.85 cattle drop on the Cargill lockout shows how concentrated beef processing has become. Fort Morgan processes 6,000 head daily from a regional draw of 200+ miles. When it goes dark, that's not just 6,000 fewer cattle processed today, it's 6,000 cattle that have to find another plant, compete for slots, and get repriced on logistics. The feeder cattle market, trading 60 days forward, discounts that backup immediately. Processing concentration means individual plant shutdowns move markets like weather events used to.

watch_list.desc: USDA Crop Progress: corn planting above 65% confirms Belt on schedule
Cargill Fort Morgan: any resolution news moves cattle $3 either direction
Weekly export sales: beans under 400K MT keeps funds in charge
Cattle on Feed: placements above 102% of year-ago signals expansion

daily_quote.text: The big money is not in the buying and selling, but in the waiting.

source_summary: CME Group, USDA reports, energy markets, agricultural news services
